# CSCI 3656-001: Numerical Computation Final Project ## Interpolation of Regular Conventional Retail Gas Prices in the United States --- ## Quinn Turner ## May 4, 2025
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).
# Importing Data - All data is in the "RawDatasets" folder - Data: - **Gas:** This is the dependent variable that will be the focus of interpolation later. - Source: U.S. Energy Information Administration (May 2025) - https://www.eia.gov/dnav/pet/hist/leafhandler.ashx?n=pet&s=emm_epm0_pte_nus_dpg&f=m - **Crude Oil Prices:** Crude oil prices cause price increases downstream, which translates to higher gas prices, so this should have some high explanatory power. - Source: U.S. Energy Information Administration (May 2025) - https://www.eia.gov/dnav/pet/pet_pri_spt_s1_d.htm - **Unemployment Rates:** Higher unemployment rates mean that fewer people will be driving, travelling, etc. This means that the demand for gas will decrease and the prices will likely also decrease. - Source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (May 2025) - https://data.bls.gov/timeseries/LNS14000000 - **CPI**: Represents the percent change betweent the average price of goods from the current period compared to the past period. When CPI increases, gas prices will likely increase. - Source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (May 2025) - https://data.bls.gov/pdq/SurveyOutputServlet - **Crude Oil Production**: The crude oil production within the U.S. in thousands of gallons per day. A higher amount of oil produced domestically will probably result in lower prices because the supply chain is shortened and tarriffs etc. will be evaded. - Source: U.S. Energy Information Administration (May 2025) - https://www.eia.gov/dnav/pet/hist/leafhandler.ashx?n=pet&s=mcrfpus2&f=m 
# Reading data
gas = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/RawDatasets/GPNC.csv')

# Renaming columns
gas["Week"] = pd.to_datetime(gas["Week of"])
gas["Price/Gallon"] = gas["Weekly U.S. Regular Conventional Retail Gasoline Prices Dollars per Gallon"]
gas.drop("Weekly U.S. Regular Conventional Retail Gasoline Prices Dollars per Gallon", axis=1, inplace=True)
gas.drop("Week of", axis=1, inplace=True)

# Getting the weeks where gas prices are missing
print(gas.loc[gas["Price/Gallon"].isnull()])

# Dropping NaN at the beginning of the dataset
gas.drop(gas.loc[gas["Week"] <= "1991-01-14"].index, inplace=True)

# Reading file
file_path = '/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/RawDatasets/COPriceNC.xls'

# Reading the header of the excel file
header = pd.read_excel(file_path, sheet_name='Data 1', header=None, nrows=2)
series_codes = header.iloc[0, 1:].tolist()
date_label = header.iloc[1, 0]

# Reading in the data
crude_price = pd.read_excel(file_path, sheet_name='Data 1', header=None, skiprows=2)
crude_price.columns = [date_label] + series_codes

# Fixing formatting and renaming features
crude_price[date_label] = pd.to_datetime(
  crude_price[date_label],
  format='%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S',
  errors='coerce'
)

crude_price.set_index(date_label, inplace=True)
crude_price = crude_price.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
crude_price["Price/Gallon"] = crude_price['Data 1: Crude Oil']
crude_price["Week"] = crude_price.index
crude_price.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
crude_price.drop(columns=["Data 1: Crude Oil"], axis=1, inplace=True)
crude_price = crude_price.drop(crude_price.columns[0], axis=1)
crude_price = crude_price[crude_price['Week'].notna()].reset_index(drop=True)
crude_price.drop(crude_price.loc[crude_price["Week"] <= '1991-01-14'].index, inplace=True)

# Converting the price from barrels to gallons
crude_price['Price/Gallon'] = crude_price['Price/Gallon']/42
crude_price.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

1      0.573333 1991-01-25

# Reading data
unemployment = pd.read_excel('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/RawDatasets/URNC.xlsx')

# The excel file uses months for columns, so it has to be melted into 2 columns
unemployment = unemployment.melt(
  id_vars='Year',
  var_name='MonthAbbr',
  value_name='Unemployment_Rate'
)

# Converting date to the correct datetime format
unemployment['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(
  unemployment['Year'].astype(str) + '-' + unemployment['MonthAbbr'],
  format='%Y-%b'
)

# Formatting and sorting based on datetime
unemployment = (
  unemployment
  .loc[:, ['Month','Unemployment_Rate']]
  .sort_values('Month')
  .reset_index(drop=True)
)

unemployment.head()

# Reading the data
cpi = pd.read_excel('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/RawDatasets/CPINC.xlsx')

# Same melting required as unemployment df
cpi = cpi.melt(
  id_vars='Year',
  var_name='MonthAbbr',
  value_name='CPI'
)

# Correcting datetime formatting
cpi['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(
  cpi['Year'].astype(str) + '-' + cpi['MonthAbbr'],
  format='%Y-%b'
)
cpi = cpi[['Month','CPI']].sort_values('Month')

crude_production = pd.read_excel('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/RawDatasets/COProductionNC.xlsx')
crude_production = crude_production.melt(
  id_vars='Year',
  var_name='MonthAbbr',
  value_name='Production'
)

# Fix formatting
crude_production['Year'] = crude_production['Year'].astype(str).str.strip()
crude_production['MonthAbbr'] = crude_production['MonthAbbr'].str.strip()
crude_production['Production'] = (crude_production['Production']
                                  .astype(str)
                                  .str.replace(',','')
                                  .astype(float)
                                 )

# Fix datetime formatting
crude_production['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(
  crude_production['Year'] + '-' + crude_production['MonthAbbr'],
  format='%Y-%b',
  errors='coerce'
)

crude_production = (
  crude_production[['Month','Production']]
  .dropna(subset=['Month','Production'])
  .sort_values('Month')
)
crude_production.head()
         Month  Production

46  1980-02-01      8705.0

# Putting all dataframes together - Creating weekly, monthly, and yearly dataframes for different levels of analysis. - Saving as CSVs into the project folder to use for numerical methods.
gas['Week'] = pd.to_datetime(gas['Week'])
gas = gas.rename(columns = {'Price/Gallon':'Gas_Price'}).sort_values('Week')
crude_price['Week'] = pd.to_datetime(crude_price['Week'])
crude_price = crude_price.rename(columns = {'Price/Gallon':'Crude_Price'}).sort_values('Week')
unemployment['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(unemployment['Month'])
unemployment = unemployment.sort_values('Month')
cpi['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(cpi['Month'])
cpi = cpi.sort_values('Month')


# Merging Gas and Crude Price (nearest within 4 days because there is some offset)
weekly_gc = pd.merge_asof(
    gas, crude_price,
    on='Week',
    direction='nearest',
    tolerance=pd.Timedelta(days=4)
).dropna(subset=['Gas_Price','Crude_Price'])

# Merging with Unemployment
weekly_gcu = pd.merge_asof(
    weekly_gc, unemployment,
    left_on='Week', right_on='Month',
    direction='backward'
).drop(columns=['Month'])

# Merging with CPI
weekly_gcuc = pd.merge_asof(
    weekly_gcu, cpi,
    left_on='Week', right_on='Month',
    direction='backward'
).drop(columns=['Month'])

# Merging with Crude Production
weekly_full = pd.merge_asof(
    weekly_gcuc, crude_production,
    left_on='Week', right_on='Month',
    direction='backward'
).drop(columns=['Month'])


weekly_full = weekly_full.set_index('Week').sort_index()

# Creating monthly dataset
monthly_full = (
    weekly_full
    .resample('ME')
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={'Week':'Month'})
)

# Creating yearly dataset
yearly_full = (
    weekly_full
    .resample('YE')
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)
yearly_full['Year'] = yearly_full['Week'].dt.year
yearly_full = yearly_full[
    ['Year','Gas_Price','Crude_Price','Unemployment_Rate','CPI','Production']
]

print("Weekly full:\n",   weekly_full.head(),   "\n")
print("Monthly full:\n",  monthly_full.head(),  "\n")
print("Yearly full:\n",   yearly_full.head())

weekly_full.to_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/CleanDatasets/WData.csv', index=False)
monthly_full.to_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/CleanDatasets/MData.csv', index=False)

monthly['GasPrice_chg_pct'] = monthly['Gas_Price'].pct_change() * 100

ax.set_ylabel('Gas Price % Change')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Analyzing the Graphs: - Gas price has extremely similar trends to crude oil price. This is an intuitive result. - Unemployment rate and crude oil production if flipped, has similar trends to gas price, but doesn't always align. This can be seen in the scatter plots, which aren't very correlated, but do have some aligned trends. - It is difficult to see if there are important shared trends between CPI and gas prices in the original time series plot because CPI is almost linear and smooth. This is explored more in the second section, where the change in gas price by % is very similar to the % change in CPI.
# Now, applying Numerical Methods
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.interpolate import lagrange as sci_lagrange
from scipy.interpolate import CubicSpline
from scipy.interpolate import PchipInterpolator
import pandas as pd
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).

def compute_metrics(y_true, y_pred):
  """
  Compute error metrics (MSE, MAE, MAPE) between true and predicted values.
  """
  mask = np.isfinite(y_true) & np.isfinite(y_pred)
  yt = np.asarray(y_true, dtype=float)[mask]
  yp = np.asarray(y_pred, dtype=float)[mask]
  if yt.size == 0:
    return {'mse': np.nan, 'mae': np.nan, 'mape': np.nan}
  mse = np.mean((yp - yt) ** 2)
  mae = np.mean(np.abs(yp - yt))
  nonzero = yt != 0
  mape = np.mean(np.abs((yp[nonzero] - yt[nonzero]) / yt[nonzero])) * 100 if nonzero.any() else np.nan
  return {'mse': mse, 'mae': mae, 'mape': mape}


def lagrange_interpolation(x, y, x_extra=None, y_extra=None, n_plot=500, x_label='Independent variable', plot=True):
  """
  Perform Lagrange interpolation and plot results.

  Parameters
  ----------
  x : array,
      Independent variable values of data
  y : array
      Dependent variable values of data
  x_extra, y_extra : array
      Extra/background points to overlay
      Helps with visualizing generalization of the interpolation
  n_plot : int, default=500
      Number of points to sample when drawing the interpolant curve.
  x_label : str, default='Independent variable'
      Label for the x-axis.
  plot : bool, default=True
      Whether to plot the results.
  Returns poly, metrics
  """
  x = np.asarray(x, dtype=float)
  y = np.asarray(y, dtype=float)
  idx = np.argsort(x)
  x, y = x[idx], y[idx]

  poly = sci_lagrange(x, y)
  x_fit = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), n_plot)
  y_fit = poly(x_fit)

  metrics = {'mse': None, 'mae': None, 'mape': None}
  # If there are extra values, you can compute the metrics on unseen data
  if x_extra is not None and y_extra is not None:
    xe = np.asarray(x_extra, dtype=float)
    ye = np.asarray(y_extra, dtype=float)
    y_pred = poly(xe)
    metrics = compute_metrics(ye, y_pred)

  if plot:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8,4))
    ax.plot(x_fit, y_fit, label='Lagrange/Newton', linestyle='--', linewidth=2)
    ax.scatter(x, y, label='Sample points')
    if x_extra is not None and y_extra is not None:
      ax.scatter(xe, ye, label='Extra data', alpha=0.6)
    ax.set_xlabel(x_label)
    ax.set_ylabel('Gas Price')
    ax.set_title('Lagrange/Newton Interpolation')
    ax.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

  return poly, metrics

# Since I'm not doing huge computations and Lagrange and Newton's generate
# the same polynomial, it is reasonable to use the same function here.
newton_interpolation = lagrange_interpolation

def spline_interpolation(x, y, x_extra=None, y_extra=None, n_plot=500, x_label='Independent variable', plot=True):
  """
  Perform cubic spline interpolation and plot results.

  Parameters
  ----------
  x : array,
    Independent variable values of data
  y : array
    Dependent variable values of data
  x_extra, y_extra : array
    Extra/background points to overlay
    Helps with visualizing generalization of the interpolation
  n_plot : int, default=500
    Number of points to sample when drawing the interpolant curve.
  x_label : str, default='Independent variable'
    Label for the x-axis.
  plot : bool, default=True
    Whether to plot the results.
  Returns cs, metrics
  """
  x = np.asarray(x, dtype=float)
  y = np.asarray(y, dtype=float)
  idx = np.argsort(x)
  x, y = x[idx], y[idx]

  cs = CubicSpline(x, y)
  x_fit = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), n_plot)
  y_fit = cs(x_fit)

  metrics = {'mse': None, 'mae': None, 'mape': None}
  if x_extra is not None and y_extra is not None:
    xe = np.asarray(x_extra, dtype=float)
    ye = np.asarray(y_extra, dtype=float)
    y_pred = cs(xe)
    metrics = compute_metrics(ye, y_pred)

  if plot:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8,4))
    ax.plot(x_fit, y_fit, label='Spline', linewidth=2)
    ax.scatter(x, y, label='Sample points')
    if x_extra is not None and y_extra is not None:
      ax.scatter(xe, ye, label='Extra data', alpha=0.6)
    ax.set_xlabel(x_label)
    ax.set_ylabel('Gas Price')
    ax.set_title('Cubic Spline Interpolation')
    ax.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

  return cs, metrics


def hermite_interpolation(x, y, x_extra=None, y_extra=None, n_plot=500, x_label='Independent variable', plot=True):
  """
  Perform Hermite interpolation and plot results.

  Parameters
  ----------
  x : array,
    Independent variable values of data
  y : array
    Dependent variable values of data
  x_extra, y_extra : array
    Extra/background points to overlay
    Helps with visualizing generalization of the interpolation
  n_plot : int, default=500
    Number of points to sample when drawing the interpolant curve.
  x_label : str, default='Independent variable'
    Label for the x-axis.
  plot : bool, default=True
    Whether to plot the results.
  Returns herm, metrics
  """
  x = np.asarray(x, dtype=float)
  y = np.asarray(y, dtype=float)
  idx = np.argsort(x)
  x, y = x[idx], y[idx]

  herm = PchipInterpolator(x, y)
  x_fit = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), n_plot)
  y_fit = herm(x_fit)

  metrics = {'mse': None, 'mae': None, 'mape': None}
  if x_extra is not None and y_extra is not None:
    xe = np.asarray(x_extra, dtype=float)
    ye = np.asarray(y_extra, dtype=float)
    y_pred = herm(xe)
    metrics = compute_metrics(ye, y_pred)

  if plot:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8,4))
    ax.plot(x_fit, y_fit, label='Hermite', linewidth=2)
    ax.scatter(x, y, label='Sample points')
    if x_extra is not None and y_extra is not None:
      ax.scatter(xe, ye, label='Extra data', alpha=0.6)
    ax.set_xlabel(x_label)
    ax.set_ylabel('Gas Price')
    ax.set_title('Piecewise Cubic Hermite (PCHIP)')
    ax.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

  return herm, metrics


def get_sample_indices(x, n, method='random', seed=None):
  """
  Gets indicies from x that correspond to a type of sampling.

  Parameters
  ----------
  x : array
    Independent variable values of data
  n : int
    Number of samples to select
  method : str ('random', 'equally', or 'chebyshev')
    Determines the sampling method
  seed : int, default=None
    Seed for reproducibility
  Returns array
  """
  length = len(x)
  if method == 'random':
    if seed is not None:
      np.random.seed(seed)
    return np.random.choice(length, size=n, replace=False)
  elif method == 'equally':
    idx = np.linspace(0, length - 1, n)
    return np.round(idx).astype(int)
  elif method == 'chebyshev':
    k = np.arange(1, n+1)
    a, b = 0, length - 1
    nodes = 0.5*(a+b) + 0.5*(b-a)*np.cos((2*k-1)/(2*n)*np.pi)
    return np.round(nodes).astype(int)
  else:
    raise ValueError(f"Unknown method: {method}")


def feature_based_tests(yearly_df, monthly_df, features, n_values, methods=('random','equally','chebyshev'), seed=0, plot=False):
  """
  For each feature, spacing method, and sample size n, compute MSE, MAE, MAPE
  for Lagrange/Newton, Spline, and Hermite interpolations, returning a DataFrame.

  Parameters
  ----------
  yearly_df : pandas DataFrame
    Yearly data.
  monthly_df : pandas DataFrame
    Monthly data.
  features : list of str
    Features to test.
  n_values : list of int
    Sample sizes to test.
  methods : list of str, default=('random','equally','chebyshev')
    Sampling methods to test.
  seed : int, default=0
    Seed for reproducibility.
  plot : bool, default=False
    Whether to plot the results.
  Returns pandas DataFrame
  """
  records = []
  for spacing in methods:
    for n in n_values:
      for feat in features:
        x_all = yearly_df[feat].values
        y_all = yearly_df['Gas_Price'].values
        idx = get_sample_indices(x_all, n, method=spacing, seed=seed)
        x = x_all[idx]; y = y_all[idx]

        _, metrics_ln = lagrange_interpolation(
          x, y,
          monthly_df[feat].values, monthly_df['Gas_Price'].values,
          plot=plot
        )
        _, metrics_sp = spline_interpolation(
          x, y,
          monthly_df[feat].values, monthly_df['Gas_Price'].values,
          plot=plot
        )
        _, metrics_he = hermite_interpolation(
          x, y,
          monthly_df[feat].values, monthly_df['Gas_Price'].values,
          plot=plot
        )

        for method, metrics in [
          ('lagrange_newton', metrics_ln),
          ('spline', metrics_sp),
          ('hermite', metrics_he)
        ]:
          records.append({
            'feature': feat,
            'spacing': spacing,
            'n': n,
            'method': method,
            'mse': metrics['mse'],
            'mae': metrics['mae'],
            'mape': metrics['mape'],
          })
  return pd.DataFrame.from_records(records)

# Making a color map to make sure graphs are easily visible.
cmap = plt.get_cmap('tab10')
palette = {
  'lagrange_newton': cmap(0),
  'spline': cmap(1),
  'hermite': cmap(2)
}
monthly_color = cmap(3)


def plot_error_vs_n(df, feature, spacing='chebyshev'):
  """
  Plot MSE vs n using a soft pastel palette for each method.

  Parameters
  ----------
  df : pandas DataFrame
    Data to plot.
  feature : str
    Feature to plot.
  spacing : str, default='chebyshev'
    Sampling method.
  Returns None
  """
  sub = df[(df['feature']==feature) & (df['spacing']==spacing)]
  methods = ['lagrange_newton', 'spline', 'hermite']
  plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
  for m in methods:
    grp = sub[sub['method']==m]
    plt.plot(grp['n'], grp['mse'], marker='o', label=m.replace('_','/').title(), color=palette[m])
  plt.title(f'{feature}: MSE vs. n ({spacing})')
  plt.xlabel('Sample size (n)')
  plt.ylabel('MSE')
  plt.legend()
  plt.tight_layout()
  plt.show()


def plot_all_interpolations(feature, n=5, spacing='chebyshev', seed=42):
  """
  Overlay all interpolations with a pastel palette and monthly data.

  Parameters
  ----------
  feature : str
    Feature to plot.
  n : int, default=5
    Sample size.
  spacing : str, default='chebyshev'
    Sampling method.
  seed : int, default=42
    Seed for reproducibility.
  Returns None
  """
  x_all = yearly[feature].values
  y_all = yearly['Gas_Price'].values
  idx = get_sample_indices(x_all, n, method=spacing, seed=seed)
  x, y = x_all[idx], y_all[idx]

  poly, _ = lagrange_interpolation(x, y, plot=False)
  cs, _ = spline_interpolation(x, y, plot=False)
  herm, _ = hermite_interpolation(x, y, plot=False)

  x_fit = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 300)
  y_vals = {
    'lagrange_newton': poly(x_fit),
    'spline': cs(x_fit),
    'hermite': herm(x_fit)
  }

  plt.figure(figsize=(7,4))
  plt.scatter(monthly[feature], monthly['Gas_Price'], color='black', alpha=0.4, s=8, label='Monthly data')
  plt.scatter(x, y, color='k', s=50, label=f'Samples (n={n}, {spacing})')

  linestyles = {'lagrange_newton': '--', 'spline': '-', 'hermite': '-.'}
  for m, yv in y_vals.items():
    plt.plot(x_fit, yv, linestyle=linestyles[m], linewidth=2, color=palette[m], label=m.replace('_','/').title())
    plt.title(f'{feature}: Interpolations (n={n}, {spacing})')
    plt.xlabel(feature)
    plt.ylabel('Gas Price')
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
  plt.show()


monthly = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/CleanDatasets/MData.csv')
yearly = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/CleanDatasets/YData.csv')
monthly['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(monthly['Month'])
monthly['YearFrac'] = monthly['Month'].dt.year + (monthly['Month'].dt.month - 1)/12

feats = ['Crude_Price','Unemployment_Rate','CPI','Production']
n_values = [5,7,9,11] # Number of points to be used
methods = ['random','equally','chebyshev']
PLOT = False

results_df = feature_based_tests(yearly, monthly, feats, n_values, methods, seed=42, plot=PLOT)

# Creating a table comparing all of the different choices for the models
results_p_table = results_df.pivot_table(
  index=['feature','spacing','n'],
  columns='method',
  values=['mse','mae','mape'])

def find_best_n_per_feature(df, spacing='chebyshev', metric='mse'):
  """
  Return, for each feature and each method under the specified spacing,
  the sample size n that minimizes the given metric.

  Parameters
  ----------
  df : pandas DataFrame
    Data to analyze.
  spacing : str, default='chebyshev'
    Sampling method.
  metric : str, default='mse'
    Metric to minimize.
  Returns a dict
  """
  df_ch = df[df['spacing'] == spacing]
  data = []
  for feat in df_ch['feature'].unique():
    subf = df_ch[df_ch['feature'] == feat]
    row = {'feature': feat}
    for method in ['lagrange_newton', 'spline', 'hermite']:
      subm = subf[subf['method'] == method]
      idx = subm[metric].idxmin()
      row[f'{method}_n'] = int(subm.loc[idx, 'n'])
      row[f'{method}_{metric}'] = subm.loc[idx, metric]
    data.append(row)
  result_df = pd.DataFrame(data)
  result_df.set_index('feature', inplace=True)
  return result_df


results_df = feature_based_tests(yearly, monthly, feats, n_values, methods, seed=42)
find_best_n_per_feature(results_df, spacing='chebyshev', metric='mse').head()

Production            0.810590         11     0.694378  
def plot_best_interpolations_per_feature(results_df, yearly_df, monthly_df, features, spacing='chebyshev', metric='mse'):
  """
  Plot the best interpolations for each feature under the specified spacing.

  Parameters
  ----------
  results_df : pandas DataFrame
    Data to analyze.
  yearly_df : pandas DataFrame
    Yearly data.
  monthly_df : pandas DataFrame
    Monthly data.
  features : list of str
    Features to plot.
  spacing : str, default='chebyshev'
    Sampling method.
  metric : str, default='mse'
    Metric to minimize.
  Returns None
  """
  best = find_best_n_per_feature(results_df, spacing, metric)
  for feat in features:
    for method in ['lagrange_newton','spline','hermite']:
      n = best.loc[feat, f'{method}_n']
      print(f"Plotting {method} for {feat} at n={n}")
      plot_all_interpolations(feat, n=n, spacing=spacing)

plot_best_interpolations_per_feature(results_df, yearly, monthly, feats)

def compare_average_errors(df):
  """
  Compute average MSE, MAE, MAPE grouped by spacing, by method, and by sample size n.

  Parameters
  ----------
  df : pandas DataFrame
    Data to analyze.
  Returns three pandas DataFrames for spacing, method, and n.
  """
  metrics = ['mse', 'mae', 'mape']
  avg_by_spacing = df.groupby('spacing')[metrics].mean().sort_index()
  method_order = ['lagrange_newton', 'spline', 'hermite']
  avg_by_method = df.groupby('method')[metrics].mean().reindex(method_order)
  avg_by_n = df.groupby('n')[metrics].mean().sort_index()
  return avg_by_spacing, avg_by_method, avg_by_n

def compare_median_errors(df):
  """
  Compute average MSE, MAE, MAPE grouped by spacing, by method, and by sample size n.

  Parameters
  ----------
  df : pandas DataFrame
    Data to analyze.
  Returns three pandas DataFrames for spacing, method, and n.
  """
  metrics = ['mse', 'mae', 'mape']
  avg_by_spacing = df.groupby('spacing')[metrics].median().sort_index()
  method_order = ['lagrange_newton', 'spline', 'hermite']
  avg_by_method = df.groupby('method')[metrics].median().reindex(method_order)
  avg_by_n = df.groupby('n')[metrics].median().sort_index()
  return avg_by_spacing, avg_by_method, avg_by_n

spacing_df, method_df, n_df = compare_average_errors(results_df)
print(spacing_df)
print(method_df)
print(n_df)
spacing_df, method_df, n_df = compare_median_errors(results_df)
print(spacing_df)
print(method_df)
print(n_df)

11  967.461572  6.824440  342.482527

for feat in ['CPI','Crude_Price','Production','Unemployment_Rate']:
  plot_all_interpolations(feat, n=7, spacing='chebyshev')
import statsmodels.api as sm
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

def mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred):
  """
  Compute MSE.

  Parameters
  ----------
  y_true : array-like
    True values.
  y_pred : array-like
    Predicted values.
    Returns float
  """
  return np.mean((y_true - y_pred) ** 2)

def mean_absolute_error(y_true, y_pred):
  """
  Compute MAE.

  Parameters
  ----------
  y_true : array-like
    True values.
  y_pred : array-like
    Predicted values.
  Returns float
  """
  return np.mean(np.abs(y_true - y_pred))

def compute_regression_metrics(y_true, y_pred):
  """
  Compute MSE, MAE, and MAPE.

  Parameters
  ----------
  y_true : array-like
    True values.
  y_pred : array-like
    Predicted values.
  Returns three floats
  """
  mse  = mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred)
  mae  = mean_absolute_error(y_true, y_pred)
  # avoid division by zero
  mask = y_true != 0
  mape = np.mean(np.abs((y_pred[mask] - y_true[mask]) / y_true[mask])) * 100 if mask.any() else np.nan
  return mse, mae, mape

def clean_and_run_with_metrics(df, name):
  """
  Compute linear regression model and metrics
  Parameters
  ----------
  df : pandas DataFrame
    Data to analyze.
  name : str
    Name of the data.
  Returns None
  """
  cols = features + ['Gas_Price']
  sub = df[cols].copy()
  sub.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan, inplace=True)
  sub.dropna(inplace=True)

  X = sub[features]
  y = sub['Gas_Price']
  X = sm.add_constant(X)

  X = sub[features]
  X['const'] = 1.0

  vifs = pd.DataFrame({
    'variable': X.columns,
    'VIF': [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i) for i in range(X.shape[1])]})
  print(vifs)

  # Fit least squares
  model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()
  y_pred = model.predict(X)

  # metrics
  mse, mae, mape = compute_regression_metrics(y, y_pred)

  print(f"--- {name} Regression ---")
  print(f"R² = {model.rsquared:.4f}")
  print(f"MSE  = {mse:.4f}")
  print(f"MAE  = {mae:.4f}")
  print(f"MAPE = {mape:.4f}%")
  print(model.summary().tables[1])
  print("\n")


monthly = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/CleanDatasets/MData.csv')
yearly = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/CleanDatasets/YData.csv')

features = ['Crude_Price', 'Unemployment_Rate','CPI','Production']

clean_and_run_with_metrics(monthly, "Monthly")

clean_and_run_with_metrics(yearly, "Yearly")

CPI has the highest VIF, therefore I will drop that and then create a new model. If the new model has high VIFs, I'll drop Production.
monthly = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/CleanDatasets/MData.csv')
yearly = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/NumericalComputationProject/CleanDatasets/YData.csv')
features = ['Crude_Price', 'Unemployment_Rate', 'Production']

monthly = monthly.drop(columns=["CPI"])
clean_and_run_with_metrics(monthly, name="Monthly")
yearly = yearly.drop(columns=["CPI"])
clean_and_run_with_metrics(yearly,  name="Yearly")
